title: Rules: Fix Cursor globs rendering (YAML array vs comma-separated)
description:
## Problem
The Cursor rules renderer (`renderCursorRule`) outputs canonical frontmatter directly to .mdc format. The `globs` field is rendered as a YAML array (canonical format), but Cursor's native .mdc format expects a comma-separated string for the `globs` field.

Example — current output (wrong):
```yaml
globs:
- "*.ts"
- "*.tsx"
```

Expected Cursor native format:
```yaml
globs: "*.ts, *.tsx"
```

## Root Cause
renderCursorRule calls renderFrontmatter(meta) which marshals the canonical RuleMeta directly. RuleMeta.Globs is []string which YAML marshals as a sequence. Cursor expects a scalar string.

## Required Changes
1. In renderCursorRule(): convert meta.Globs []string to comma-separated string before rendering
2. Verify canonicalizeCursorRule() correctly splits comma-separated globs back to []string (it uses parse.ParseMDCFrontmatter which should already handle this)
3. Add round-trip test: Cursor globs → canonical → Cursor should preserve format

## Success Criteria
- [ ] renderCursorRule outputs globs as comma-separated string, not YAML array
- [ ] Round-trip test: Cursor rule with globs → canonicalize → render back to Cursor produces same format
- [ ] Existing rules tests pass
- [ ] New test case for multi-glob Cursor rule round-trip
- [ ] `make build` succeeds

## Files to Modify
- `cli/internal/converter/rules.go` (renderCursorRule)
- `cli/internal/converter/rules_test.go`

## Research Reference
docs/research/rules-research.md — Converter Coverage Audit, Recommended Action #7
close_reason: Fixed. Cursor globs now render as comma-separated string. MDC parser handles both formats. All tests pass.

title: MCP: Fix transport type mapping (http vs streamable-http)
description:
## Problem
Claude Code and Copilot CLI use "http" for streamable HTTP transport. The canonical format and most renderers use "streamable-http". This causes broken interop:
- Importing from Claude Code: "http" is preserved as-is (no mapping to canonical "streamable-http")
- Rendering to Claude Code: "streamable-http" is emitted, which may not be recognized

## Root Cause
The canonical format chose "streamable-http" as the internal representation but Claude Code (the primary provider) uses "http". No mapping exists between the two.

## Required Changes
1. In the default canonicalizer: map "http" → "streamable-http" on import (alongside existing httpUrl/serverUrl normalization)
2. In renderClaudeMCP(): map "streamable-http" → "http" on export
3. In renderCopilotMCP(): same mapping
4. In renderCursorMCP(): same mapping (Cursor follows Claude Code conventions)
5. Verify Gemini/Windsurf/others don't use "http" or need similar mapping

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Canonicalizing a Claude Code config with type:"http" produces canonical type:"streamable-http"
- [ ] Rendering to Claude Code with canonical type:"streamable-http" produces type:"http"
- [ ] Same for Copilot CLI and Cursor renders
- [ ] Existing MCP tests pass (update expected values where needed)
- [ ] New test: round-trip Claude Code config with HTTP transport type
- [ ] `make build` succeeds

## Files to Modify
- `cli/internal/converter/mcp.go` (Canonicalize default path, renderClaudeMCP, renderCopilotMCP, renderCursorMCP)
- `cli/internal/converter/mcp_test.go`

## Research Reference
docs/research/mcp-research.md — Converter Coverage Audit, Issue #3
close_reason: Fixed. Transport type mapping http↔streamable-http added to canonicalize+render. All tests pass.

title: Simulated features for non-native providers
description:
rulesync generates 'simulated' skills/commands/subagents for providers that don't natively support them, embedding prompt-engineering text in the root rule file.

RULESYNC VALIDATION: Issue #902 requests ephemeral generation (rulesync run without persisting files) — shows users want even MORE flexibility in how simulated content is delivered. But no issues praising or complaining about simulation quality, which suggests it either works quietly or isn't widely used.

Issue #900 reveals a deeper problem: path references in rules aren't transformed during generation. If a rule says 'see @.rulesync/rules/example.md', it stays as-is in the generated output — broken link. This affects simulated features too since they reference skill files by path.

SYLLAGO APPROACH: Don't simulate by default. Instead:
1. When converting content to a provider that lacks native support, emit a clear WARNING telling the user what was dropped and why
2. Offer an opt-in --simulate flag that generates prompt-engineered fallbacks
3. If we do simulate, solve the path reference problem rulesync hasn't — rewrite paths to point to the correct provider-relative locations

This keeps the default behavior honest (you know what your provider actually supports) while offering simulation as an explicit choice. Rulesync's silent simulation means users might not realize their "skills" on Copilot are just prompt suggestions, not native features.

PRIORITY: Post-release. Focus on native support coverage first.

title: M4: Hook matcher groups not whitelist-filtered before merge
description:
## Why
The hook matcher group (everything except the "event" field) is inserted as raw JSON into settings.json. Unlike MCP install (which roundtrips through a typed struct to strip unknown fields), hooks preserve ALL JSON fields including unknown ones. A crafted hook.json could inject arbitrary keys into the provider's settings.json.

## What
Parse hook matcher groups through a typed struct before merging, matching the MCP whitelist approach.

## How (general)
- Define a typed struct for hook matcher groups with known fields (matcher, command, timeout, etc.)
- In installHook(), unmarshal the raw matcher group JSON into the typed struct
- Re-marshal the struct (which strips unknown fields) before passing to sjson.SetRawBytes
- This matches the pattern already used in MCP's ExtractServerEntries

## Success Criteria
- Known hook fields (matcher, command, timeout) are preserved during install
- Unknown/unexpected JSON fields are stripped before writing to settings.json
- Installed hooks function correctly with providers

## Testing
- Unit test: hook with only known fields — all preserved
- Unit test: hook with extra unknown fields — unknown fields stripped
- Unit test: hook with all supported matcher fields — all preserved
- Golden test: compare installed hook JSON before/after whitelist filtering

## Threat Report
docs/security/threat-inventory-2026-03-26.md — Section M4
close_reason: Implemented in security hardening session 2026-03-26

title: Add release environment with manual approval gate
description:
## Why
The release workflow (release.yml) has no environment protection rules. It triggers on any v* tag push and builds+signs+publishes binaries with contents:write and id-token:write permissions. No manual approval step exists between tag push and public release.

## What
Create a "release" environment in GitHub with required manual approval, and reference it in release.yml.

## How
- Settings > Environments > New environment > "release"
- Add required reviewers (holdenhewett)
- Optionally: restrict to main branch only
- In release.yml, add: environment: release (under the job definition)

## Success Criteria
- Tag push triggers the release workflow but pauses for manual approval
- Approved releases proceed to build, sign, and publish
- Rejected releases do not publish anything

## Blocked By
Repo must be public for environment protection rules (free tier)

title: Fix data races in registry add and remove
description:
## Phase 0a: Fix data races in registry add and remove

### Problem
Both doRegistryAdd (app.go:216-258) and doRegistryRemove (app.go:191-205) capture `cfg := a.registryCfg` (a pointer) then mutate `cfg.Registries` in a goroutine. Since cfg points to a.registryCfg, View() can read the slice concurrently — data race.

### Implementation
**Strategy:** Inside each command closure, load a fresh config from disk, modify the fresh copy, save. Never touch a.registryCfg from the goroutine.

**Remove (app.go:191-205):**
1. Pass only the registry name (string) into the closure — not the cfg pointer
2. Inside closure: `freshCfg, err := config.Load(repoRoot)`
3. Filter out the registry name from freshCfg.Registries
4. Save freshCfg
5. Do filesystem removal (registry.Remove)
6. If config.Load fails, return registryRemoveDoneMsg with the error

**Add (app.go:216-258):**
1. After cloning and scanning, load fresh config inside the closure
2. Append the new registry entry to the fresh config's Registries slice
3. Save the fresh config
4. Return registryAddDoneMsg as before
5. If config.Load fails, return registryAddDoneMsg with the error

**Concurrency note:** registryOpInProgress prevents concurrent registry ops from UI, so no TOCTOU risk between Load and Save.

### Files
- `cli/internal/tui/app.go` lines 191-205 (remove) and 216-258 (add)

### Success Criteria
- [ ] Remove closure does NOT capture or reference `cfg` or `a.registryCfg` — only passes registry name as string
- [ ] Add closure does NOT mutate `a.registryCfg.Registries` — loads fresh config inside closure
- [ ] Both closures call config.Load() inside the goroutine, modify the fresh copy, and save
- [ ] config.Load failure returns done message with error (not silently swallowed)
- [ ] `make build && make test` passes
- [ ] No `go vet` race warnings (existing tests should cover this)

### DO NOT
- Do not mark this bead as complete — a verification agent will do that
- Do not modify done-message handlers (those are Phase 1d)
- Do not change the message types or their fields
close_reason: Closed

title: Redesign 'syllago convert' for provider-to-provider conversion
description:
Redesign the convert command to do true provider-to-provider conversion rather than library-to-provider rendering (which is now the 'show' command's job).

New signature: syllago convert <type> <name> --from <provider> --to <provider>

The command takes content from one provider's format and outputs it in another provider's format, going through canonical as the intermediate step (hub-and-spoke). If the source provider can be inferred (e.g., from the content's metadata), the --from flag could be optional.

This pairs with the new 'show' command (syllago-mjmy):
- show = view library content in any format (read-only, stdout)
- convert = transform between provider formats (the actual conversion tool)
- install = convert + write to provider config dir

title: Add approval flow for project-local config modifications in sandbox
description:
Project-local config directories (.claude/, .gemini/, .codex/) are mounted RW in the sandbox without any approval flow. Changes are applied directly to the real project, unlike global config paths which go through staging/diff/approval.

A sandboxed provider can silently modify project configuration (e.g. .claude/rules/) without the user seeing or approving the changes.

Options:
A) Include project-local config dirs in the staging/diff mechanism (more secure, more complex)
B) Document the limitation explicitly and add a post-sandbox summary of project config changes (simpler, still informative)

Files: cli/internal/sandbox/profile.go, cli/internal/sandbox/bwrap.go, cli/internal/sandbox/runner.go

title: Cross-provider skill hook conversion
description:
When converting Claude Code skills with hooks frontmatter to other providers, map skill-scoped hooks to the target provider's hook system where possible.

Current state: hooks and effort fields are preserved in SkillMeta (canonical format) and survive Claude-to-Claude roundtrips. For non-Claude providers, hooks are embedded as prose notes explaining they require skill-scoped hook support.

Future work:
- Map Claude skill hooks to Gemini CLI global hooks in settings.json (lose skill scoping but hooks actually execute)
- Map hook event types between providers (e.g., pre_tool_use → BeforeTool)
- Handle scoping semantics (Claude: per-skill, Gemini: global, Copilot: none)
- Decide on warning/confirmation UX when hook scoping is lost

Research notes:
- Gemini CLI has 10 hook event types, configured in settings.json, all global scope
- Copilot CLI has no hook system
- Kiro has hooks but different event model
- The core challenge is that hooks are executable behavior, not instructional text — prose embedding preserves intent but hooks won't fire
close_reason: Skill hooks surfaced as actionable warnings for providers with hook support. Providers without hooks keep prose embedding. Tests pass.

title: Close TOCTOU race in installer copyFile symlink check
description:
installer/copy.go:28-34 checks if destination is a symlink via os.Lstat(), then creates via os.Create(). Race window between check and create allows an attacker to replace the file with a symlink (e.g. to ~/.ssh/authorized_keys) between the two operations.

Fix: Use atomic replace pattern — write to temp file in same directory, then os.Rename() (which is atomic on POSIX). This eliminates the TOCTOU window entirely.

Files: cli/internal/installer/copy.go
close_reason: Closed

title: Add statusIsErr field to App struct
description:
## Phase 1a: Add statusIsErr field to App struct

### Problem
statusMessage has no error/success flag. All status messages render with successMsgStyle and "Done:" prefix — even errors like "Add failed: not a syllago registry...".

### Implementation
1. Add `statusIsErr bool` field to App struct (near statusMessage at ~line 103)
2. Set `statusIsErr = true` on all error paths in done-message handlers
3. Set `statusIsErr = false` on all success paths in done-message handlers

After Phase 1d extraction, the status-setting happens in each handler before calling rebuildRegistryState(). Find every place statusMessage is set and add the corresponding statusIsErr assignment:
- Error paths (where message contains "failed" or similar): statusIsErr = true
- Success paths: statusIsErr = false
- In-progress messages (like "Adding registry: ..."): statusIsErr = false

### Files
- `cli/internal/tui/app.go` — App struct definition and all done-message handlers

### Success Criteria
- [ ] `statusIsErr bool` field exists on App struct
- [ ] Every assignment to a.statusMessage is accompanied by an assignment to a.statusIsErr
- [ ] Error messages get statusIsErr = true
- [ ] Success messages get statusIsErr = false
- [ ] In-progress messages get statusIsErr = false
- [ ] `make build && make test` passes

### DO NOT
- Do not mark this bead as complete — a verification agent will do that
- Do not change how statusMessage is rendered yet (that's Phases 1b and 1c)
- Do not add clearing logic yet (that's Phase 3)

title: M6: Absolute paths leak through promote pipeline
description:
## Why
BundledScriptMeta.OriginalPath (in metadata.go:33) stores the absolute filesystem path at add time (e.g., /home/username/.claude/hooks/lint.sh). This field survives copyForPromote and ends up in .syllago.yaml files in public registries, revealing the original user's home directory path, username, and filesystem layout.

## What
Strip or relativize OriginalPath when promoting content to registries.

## How (general)
- In the promote pipeline's copyForPromote function, sanitize .syllago.yaml before committing to the registry branch
- Replace absolute OriginalPath with just the filename (filepath.Base) or a relative path
- Consider whether OriginalPath is even needed in the registry copy — if it's only used for local tracking, it can be removed entirely during promote
- Also audit other metadata fields for absolute path leakage

## Success Criteria
- Promoted content in registries contains no absolute filesystem paths
- No username or home directory information leaks through .syllago.yaml
- Local library copies retain OriginalPath for local use (no behavior change locally)

## Testing
- Unit test: promote content with BundledScriptMeta, verify OriginalPath is sanitized in registry copy
- Unit test: verify local library copy still has full OriginalPath
- Integration test: promote → clone registry → inspect .syllago.yaml for path leakage
- Grep test: no absolute paths matching /home/ or /Users/ in promoted output

## Threat Report
docs/security/threat-inventory-2026-03-26.md — Section M6
close_reason: Implemented in security hardening session 2026-03-26

title: Harden sandbox env var injection surface
description:
Provider profiles inject environment variables via SandboxEnvVars (runner.go:173-177) without key-name validation. While env filtering (envfilter.go) uses an allowlist for HOST env vars, profile-injected vars bypass this.

A corrupted or future profile could inject dangerous env vars like LD_PRELOAD, PYTHONPATH, NODE_PATH that load malicious modules at runtime inside the sandbox.

Fix: Add a denylist of dangerous env var names (LD_PRELOAD, LD_LIBRARY_PATH, PYTHONPATH, NODE_PATH, PERL5LIB, etc.) and validate profile SandboxEnvVars keys against it before injection.

Files: cli/internal/sandbox/runner.go, cli/internal/sandbox/envfilter.go
close_reason: Closed

title: add hooks only reads global settings.json, ignores project-level
description:
syllago add hooks --from claude-code only looks at the global ~/.claude/settings.json. If the global file doesn't exist or has no hooks, it reports 'No settings.json found' even when the project-level .claude/settings.json has hooks.

The discovery code (syllago add --from claude-code with no args) correctly finds project-level hooks. But the add-hooks code path (runAddHooks) only processes the global settings file.

Related to syllago-f4z1 (global vs project collision). Both need the hook add pathway to read project-level settings.

title: M3: Hook event name used as sjson key without validation
description:
## Why
The event name from hook JSON is used directly in "hooks." + event + ".-1" as an sjson key path. Unlike MCP server names (validated by IsValidItemName), event names aren't validated. An event name containing dots (e.g., "PreToolUse.evil.path") causes sjson to create nested JSON structures instead of flat keys, corrupting settings.json.

## What
Validate hook event names before using them as sjson key paths.

## How (general)
- Add event name validation in installHook() before constructing the sjson key path
- Option A: Validate against a whitelist of known provider event names (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, etc.)
- Option B: Ensure the event string contains no dots or special characters that sjson interprets
- Option A is preferred — it also catches typos and invalid event names early

## Success Criteria
- Hook files with invalid event names are rejected with a clear error
- Hook files with dots in event names are rejected (sjson key injection)
- All known provider event names pass validation
- Error message tells the user what valid event names look like

## Testing
- Unit test: install hook with valid event name (PreToolUse) — succeeds
- Unit test: install hook with dotted event name — rejected
- Unit test: install hook with empty event name — rejected
- Unit test: install hook with each known provider event name — all succeed

## Threat Report
docs/security/threat-inventory-2026-03-26.md — Section M3
close_reason: Implemented in security hardening session 2026-03-26

title: Hook portability report: warn about capability mismatches during install/export
description:
WHY: Different providers have vastly different hook capabilities. A hook using updatedInput (arg rewriting) only works on Claude Code and Gemini CLI — Windsurf/Cursor silently ignore the rewrite. A hook using 'prompt' handler type only works on Claude Code. Users exporting hooks cross-provider get no warning about these capability losses.

RESEARCH REF: docs/providers/audit/hooks.md (capability matrix on lines 46-59)
RESEARCH REF: docs/providers/claude-code/hooks.md (4 handler types)
RESEARCH REF: docs/providers/windsurf/hooks.md (command-only handlers)

WHAT TO BUILD:
- During syllago install/export of hooks, analyze hook entries for provider-specific features
- Warn about: handler types not supported by target (http/prompt/agent → command-only providers), updatedInput not supported (only CC/Gemini), async execution not supported, matcher syntax differences
- Could be part of the broader syllago compat command (syllago-u6b6) or standalone

CAPABILITY MATRIX TO ENCODE:
| Capability | CC | Gemini | Windsurf | Cursor | Cline | Copilot | Codex | Kiro |
| Block tool calls | Y | Y | Y | Y (3/6) | Y (PreToolUse) | Y (preToolUse) | N | Y (CLI) |
| Modify args | Y (updatedInput) | Y (rewrite) | N | N | Y (context inject) | Y | N | N |
| Handler types | cmd,http,prompt,agent | cmd | cmd | cmd | executable | cmd | cmd | askAgent+cmd |
| Async execution | Y | N | N | N | N | N | N | N |

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
- Installing a hook with 'type: http' to Windsurf produces a warning
- Installing a hook with updatedInput to Cursor produces a warning
- No false warnings for compatible features

TESTING: Unit tests for each warning condition

title: 8.2: SCAFFOLD — Update TestAddWizard forward-path test shell indices
description:
Update the existing TestAddWizard_ValidateStep_Forward test in cli/internal/tui/wizard_invariant_test.go to use shellIndexForStep() instead of literal integers, since the enum shift (addStepReview 3→4, addStepExecute 4→5) invalidates the old raw values.

## What to do

In TestAddWizard_ValidateStep_Forward (and any other forward-path tests), replace:
```go
m.shell.SetActive(3)  // old Review index
m.shell.SetActive(4)  // old Execute index
```

With:
```go
m.step = addStepReview
m.shell.SetActive(m.shellIndexForStep(addStepReview))
m.validateStep()

m.reviewAcknowledged = true
m.step = addStepExecute
m.shell.SetActive(m.shellIndexForStep(addStepExecute))
m.validateStep()
```

Search the entire file for literal shell.SetActive(N) with N > 0 and replace them all with shellIndexForStep calls.

## Success criteria
- `cd cli && go test ./internal/tui/... -run TestAddWizard_ValidateStep_Forward` -> pass
- `grep -n "shell.SetActive([0-9])" cli/internal/tui/wizard_invariant_test.go` -> returns no matches (all literal-integer calls replaced)

title: Phase 3: Wire all CLI commands to use structured errors
description:
Parent feature for wiring all ~196 fmt.Errorf calls in cli/cmd/syllago/*.go to use output.NewStructuredError or output.NewStructuredErrorDetail.

Design doc: docs/plans/2026-03-23-structured-error-system.md

Conversion rules:
1. Terminal errors (no %w): use NewStructuredError(code, message, suggestion)
2. Wrapped errors (%w): use NewStructuredErrorDetail(code, message, suggestion, err.Error())
3. Return the StructuredError directly (Pattern B) — printExecuteError in main.go handles it
4. Only convert user-facing errors — internal %w wraps that bubble up from packages are converted at the command layer

After completing all sub-beads, run a verification agent to confirm no plain fmt.Errorf remains for user-facing errors in any command file.
close_reason: All structured errors wired, tests pass
